data.text:
INT: Part 4, interview with Mark Verstandig, running tape 17. 

Last time, we ended in-- on your account of your meeting with the policemen in Warsaw. 

MV: Yes. I remember the policeman, there was a heavy-- a heavy person behind me. And he put his hand on my shoulder. I turned around. And he told me-- and he told me, you're a Jew. You're coming with me. 

So I told him that, first of all, you are too familiar. I took off his hand from my shoulder. And I told him we didn't feed pigs together on a farm. If you want to address me, be polite. 

And he said to me, look. And besides this, I told him, I am not a Jew, and please do me a favor. Fuck off. 

He said, look, I have met a lot of Jews. I dispatched them to hell. But I have never met a-- a chutzpah-- what do you call it in? Such a-- 

INT: Audacity? 

MV: In such an audacious-- such an audacious Jew. And I told him, look, I'm not a Jew. And-- and I think you should get lost. 

Look at my satchel that I was-- I was carrying a Hessian-- a-- a-- a Hessian briefcase, packed with books, very heavy, of course, from a whole week. I told him, look under the books. There's some hand grenades. And there are people with me, behind me, in front of me. 

And therefore, if you want-- if you want me to go with you to the station, by all means, let's go. But I can assure you, you will never reach the station. You will lose your life before you reach the station. We don't want to carry out executions on the site. But if you force our hands, can't be helped. Now let's go. 

Said-- he was as white as a ghost. He said, what should I do? I said, do you want from me a little advice? I'll tell you what you should do. I'll show you a piece of paper. And you will read the paper, and you will salute and turn around and go away. 

What should I tell the other people, the people who is-- who, who, what shall I tell the men who pointed-- who pointed out to me that you are a Jew and he is observing us? Tell him that I am a Gestapo officer. And take him in to a doorway and bloody his nose that, that he embarrassed you so much. 

He went away. I should say that, but I was shocked as never before, the first time I was recognized as a Jew. And I realized that it was a moment of a bluff which succeeded. But I could have lost my life, and my friends and my wife never-- nobody would have known where my remains are. 

And I decided, at that time, that eventually, eventually I will be recognized, because the war was not going to be over overnight, in a few days. And sooner or later, somebody will recognize me. And not every policeman will be such a fool like this old one. And that will be the end of it. 

So that was one day where I was really very, very shocked. Then I came back to my friends' flat. Of course, at that time, I came back over for two days-- for-- for two days in, in Warsaw. 

I didn't tell them the story. But I had bad news. The news was that my wife is arriving next day in the morning. She would arrive next day in the morning in Warsaw with my friend Kubick, that she was thrown out from her safe place. 

And I went over there, next day in the morning. And there, I found out what happened. She was just thrown out from the safe place given to her, arranged for her by the leader of the AK, by the commander of the-- of the AK for the whole district. 

They just opened the door and told her, look, we don't want to keep you anymore. You go to the Kubicks' with your friend. And they showed even how to go there, this way. 

They didn't rob her. They didn't take anything from her. They, they, they were paid, by the way. But they didn't do any harm to her. But they told her, we don't want to keep you. 

INT: Who were these people? 

MV: These people are-- were forest rangers. And they were members of the AK. Their daughter was working at the post office. And she was a member of the AK. She was the liaison-- the liaison officer of the AK. 

And then I was just wondering what happened. That much I'll talk about some later. But now, first of all, let me concentrate on my wife's arrival in Warsaw. 

It happened that Kubick came with her, my friends from Poland came with her to Warsaw, to where he has family. And the family agreed that they will take her in. 

But the moment they came down from the train, they took a rickshaw. You know what a rickshaw is. It's a bicycle with arrangements for two people and-- and luggage. But the rickshaw-- young rickshaw man, they gave him the address. But the young rickshaw man took them straight to the police station. He immediately recognized my wife, that they are Jews. 

At the police station, my wife denied that she is Jewish. And they ask her all kinds of questions of, of the Catholic liturgy. And she answers the questions very well. And-- but they still insisted that she is a Jew. 

The man in charge at that time, the man in charge, he says that-- that she is Jewish. And he told her, look, you came-- you, you-- and he started first of all to check what she is wearing around to-- a body check. And they found-- he found a lot of dollars, gold dollars, jewelry, and-- and dollars, bank notes, a lot of money, a lot-- lot of money, thousands of dollars. 

Now, she is very-- he had put everything on the table. He said, now, the wife came back to the husband with the money. The husband is already in-- in Warsaw. And then the husband will not even know that she died in the cellar of the Gestapo. 

And my wife told him, look, first of all, I'm not Jewish. And secondly, the Gestapo will not prove that I am Jewish-- that I am Jewish. They may take the money. And they will let me go. 

Again, he said, well, what should I do? He said, look, let's divide it. She made a mistake. She was-- she-- and divide it, so he said, it's all right. 

So he, he divided it honestly, $1 for $1, a-- and a gold coin for a gold coin, jewelry, and so on. His half he put immediately into the drawer. And then from-- he took her-- because she had some local money, probably zlotys. He took about 400 or 500, no, 1,000 zlotys, 1,000 zlotys, which was about $3, $2 or $3, $2 or $3 and left them on the table. 

He called in two policemen and told them, look, [SIGHS] this young lady, she left us $1,000. I give her back what she has got here on the table. And I think we should call a-- [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a cab-- a cab and to let them go. 

It happened also that in the other room, they investigated Kubick. And they thought that he is Jewish. He asked all kinds of questions. And then, when it was a question of a vesper or something like that, he didn't know what they were asking. 

And at that time, they told them to let his-- his trousers down. So they realized that he is not-- he is not Jewish. And he told them, look, gentlemen. I studied to be a priest. What you are talking about, the name of the vesper may be used in another-- may be-- may be-- may be-- may be-- used somewhere else, but not in, in, in, Galicia or not in [NON-ENGLISH] and so on and so on. 

They immediately had the suspicion that he is an AK officer and they were scared of him. And they let-- they called then a cab. And he gave an advice to my wife, never use a rickshaw. They are all informers. Take a-- take a cab with an elderly driver, an elderly coach driver. 

And that was the end of this incident. We came-- we discussed the matter of state in Warsaw. She realized that she will not be able to go out. And the people-- the, the family of my friend Kubick had a very small flat, that was impossible to make a hiding place over there. That means she would have to live open. And with her looks, she wouldn't have survived a single day. 

So they decided that she should go back. And he took her back. And I said to Kubick that I will come in a few days' time, because I was shocked with this incident, what happened before with the policeman. 

And so I came back to my friends. And I told them, look, I'll tell you what happened to my wife. I'll tell you what happened to me with the policeman, because I didn't tell them before. 

The only way of surviving in Warsaw for me would be to join the AK and to go around the city in company of AK. I know how they operate. One goes ahead. They never go two people together. Behind is one and one is in the front. They keep an eye on each other. 

And so they walk around the streets of Warsaw. I speak German like all Germans. I may be of use to go around to a cafe where the Germans are sitting and to listen to their conversations and so on and so on. I'm [INAUDIBLE]. I want to join official the AK. 

They didn't-- they were not so happy about it. But they told me that they are going to bring up the matter at the highest level. And that shocked me. 

Why at the highest level? A young man speaks German, he doesn't look Jewish, speaks German, speaks Polish. Why should it be a question of contacting the highest level to join the AK? 

And how, a day later, they told me that the man they wanted to talk to is-- he's not around. And the other man is not around. They will come tomorrow, come after. 

After a few days, I asked them, look, tell me the truth. They told me that the truth is truth is that AK is not taking in Jews. They have got a few Jews here and there in order to show-- to, to, to create a kind of an alibi for overseas, that there are a few-- there are few Jews here and there. But AK doesn't take Jews in. 

INT: Are we talking about 1943 now? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: 1943 now, is that the year? 

MV: That was-- no, that was the end of 1942. That was end of-- that's December '92. They don't take Jews. 

And it's unfortunate. As a matter of fact, he told me-- he told me some moneys, some money will be coming in for Jews, because there are some Jews connected with the Delegatura, for the supporting Jews. Look, a part of it will go-- a part of it will go to those people to distribute amongst the Jews who are in hiding. But the-- but the most of it will be swallowed by the AK. 

That is the position. I can't change it. But that is the truth and the whole truth. We talked about this. We talked later on with-- we were drinking Bimber for a good few hours, talking about politics, Eisenhower, [NON-ENGLISH] in Africa and so on. 

And I said, look, in such a case, I will go. I'll go back. I'm not-- I'm not-- I can't-- I can't work the city. I lost confidence in myself. I can't work the city force anymore. 

Now, that was that. Few days later, I was on my way. And I was always thinking, how is it possible that my wife was thrown out from a safe place, from a safe house, supplied by-- provided for her by the commander of AK, Jasinszki. 

By the way, one of the AK people in Warsaw told my friends over there, if he is such a pull with Jedrus, why doesn't he give him a job over there in his place? Didn't like it. But I was thinking about Jedrus myself. What's going on? 

I knew that he was an antisemite. I knew that his father was an antisemite. But after all, he approached me. I didn't come-- I didn't come to him begging him for protection and so on. 

He offered me protection. He offered protection for my wife. So without his approval-- I was thinking without his approval, [NON-ENGLISH]-- the family name of those foresters was [NON-ENGLISH]. The family [NON-ENGLISH] wouldn't have dared to open the door. 

At the same time, they didn't do Jan any, any harm. That means it must have been the instruction of Jasinszki. 

INT: How do you explain-- 

MV: And I was-- beg pardon? 

INT: How do you explain this? 

MV: But I didn't-- I thought that he was an antisemite, and his father was antisemite. And after a few months, he regretted that he gave a safe house to a Jewess. He regretted. 

But he didn't want to do any, any harm. He said, look, send her to the Kubicks. The Kubicks are their friends. They will take care of her. And don't do any harm to her. 

That's what I thought. What I didn't know at that time-- I mean, accusing in my mind Jasinszki, Jedrus Jasinszki, of antisemitism what I didn't know is that Jasinszki gave her a-- a safe house and kept her there, disobeying an order of the chief, of the commander in chief of the AK for the whole country. 

That was an order issued by General Bor-Komorowski, was the chief commander of the AK for the whole Poland. And the order was number 116, issued on the 15th of September 1942. And in that order, General Komorowski, Bor-Komorowski, ordered to kill Jews who escaped from ghetto and to kill Russians who escape from the POW camps-- to kill Russian POWs who escaped from the camp-- from, from the German camp, and-- but in the first place, the Jews. 

Consequently, Jasinszki for a few months was disobeying an order of his commander-in-chief in Warsaw. And after a few months, he decided, look, he can't go on like that, being a soldier and disobeying orders. So he-- he certainly issued an order to the [NON-ENGLISH], let her go, but don't do-- don't do any harm. Send her back to the Kubicks. That was my explanation later when I learned about this infamous order of General Bor-Komorowski, the-- from the AK. 

INT: What was the reason for this order? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: What was the reason for this order? 

MV: To get rid of the Jews. As long-- as long as they didn't know that the Jews were-- that the-- about this final-- about this final solution, they didn't do any harm to the Jews. But the moment the Germans started mass killing of the Jews, they didn't want any Jews to survive, just like that. 

No matter what Mr. Feliks says, he aren't there-- or what Tygodnik Polski say. They-- they would be-- they, they would be happy if I would have written in my book at the Poles, that the-- that the AK and the-- the underground, they all were like the Danish. The-- the Jews were evacuated. But when they came back, they opened the doors. Their flats were in, in best-- in good order. Flowers were prepared for them. 

You see, they would-- I mean, Mr. Feliks from the Tygodnik Polski would like me to write about it. But that was not the truth. The truth was that they wanted to get rid of the Jews. And there are documents. 

And I repeated it. I think I mentioned this earlier. There are documents in the Yad Vashem stating, the Delagatura-- the Delagatura policy was that Jews should not come back to their, their homes, to their factories, to their-- to-- to-- to their trade, to their flats. If they come back, they will be treated-- they will be treated like invaders. It will not be a restitution. They-- and the-- and the Poles will, will force them out, will keep them in a-- will keep them in a closed area until they-- until they emigrated. 

Now, that was the position. It was not a question. It was not a question of, as you mentioned, whether they were alien, whether the Poles, they considered the Jews as an alien element. The Jews in Poland were not-- were, were not worse than the Jews in Hungary, than the Jews in Bulgaria, than the Jews in Czechoslovakia, than the Jews anywhere else. But that was the position. 

INT: What, what was behind the thinking in-- what was behind the thinking of the AK, upper echelons in agreeing with the German directive? 

MV: It just an occasion, an occasion to get rid of the Jews and to-- and to grab their-- their houses, their flats, their factories, and they-- and-- and to get-- to get rid of a minority, just like that. 

INT: What-- what-- or actually, where-- where did the AK, as an underground force, where did it stand in the spectrum of the Polish underground forces? Was it left or right? Where, where was it in the spectrum? 

MV: No. That-- and that was the mainstream. The AK was the mainstream. They were Socialist over there. And they were [NON-ENGLISH] people, [NON-ENGLISH] people, and so on. They were-- this was the mainstream. 

The few Jews who were in the AK were in units which was solid-- which were exclusive Socialist units-- PPS unit, like the ALP here, and the-- and the-- the Social Democrats unit. They kept a few Jews. 

The only-- the only underground which kept Jews was Armia Ludowa. That was a Communist underground, which kept Jews. You see, even in Warsaw, in many-- in-- in-- in-- in many memoirs, you see, you will find it that in Warsaw during, during the Uprising, when people came out and they wanted to join the AK, the AK told them, we are not keeping, so you go to the Armia Ludowa. They make the-- they may take you in. We are-- we don't take Jews. 

That was the mainstream under the Delegatura. And the Delegatura was the delegation of the-- of the government in-- of the government in exile. The only-- you asked what was the reason. The reason was just to grab the little house, the flat, the little factory or the larger factory, which belongs to the Jews. That was the only reason. 

INT: Were there any other underground organizations? 

MV: No. There was only the mainstream organization. That is the AK. And on the left side, there was the Armia Ludowa. It's the Communist one. And they accepted Jews. 

And then on the extreme right, was NSZ. This is Narodowe Sily Zbrojne. Yeah. And they were just Hitlerites. Very interesting, because there was-- one of their-- one of their divisions or a few divisions of their who retreated together with the Germans. 

OK. And yes. That was more or less everything. NSZ on this-- but by the way, at a later stage, NSZ joined the AK under Bor-Komorowski. So the NSZ became part of the-- of-- of the-- of the AK. 

INT: So after Warsaw, what did you decide to do? 

MV: After Warsaw, I came back and-- to Polaniec, came back by train, by-- to-- to Polaniec. And my friend Kubick took me to the place where he placed my wife. And that was [NON-ENGLISH], in [NON-ENGLISH], Kolonia, in a forest, just a clearing in a forest. 

There were two houses there. And we went by night. It was cold. It was snow. And we went over there. And there was a cottage, a small cottage. 

The family's name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't remember his, his-- the Christian name of the-- of this [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyhow, family [NON-ENGLISH]. 

They had a small house, a room, a living room, which was at the same time a bedroom, and a kitchen. And my wife was there. They-- they have-- they had a few-- they had two sons. 

The sons were-- were transported to the cow shed. And they kept my wife quite open, but inside. She didn't go out. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
